examples.explanation: Array: [1, 101, 2, 3, 100]. Start at index 0 with last = -∞. Include 1 (since 1 > -∞): recurse from index 1 with last=1. At index 1, include 101 (101 > 1): recurse from index 2 with last=101. At index 2, skip 2 (2 not > 101), recurse from index 3 with last=101. At index 3, skip 3, recurse from index 4 with last=101. At index 4, skip 100, reach end: return 0. Backtrack: sum at index 4 = 0, index 3 = 0, index 2 = 0, index 1 = 101 + 0 = 101, index 0 = 1 + 101 = 102. Alternative at index 1: skip 101, recurse from index 2 with last=1. Include 2 (2 > 1): recurse from index 3 with last=2. Include 3 (3 > 2): recurse from index 4 with last=3. Include 100 (100 > 3): recurse from index 5 (end): return 0. Backtrack: sum = 100 + 0 = 100 at index 4, 3 + 100 = 103 at index 3, 2 + 103 = 105 at index 2, 0 at index 1 (skip), 1 + 105 = 106 at index 0. Maximum is 106 from subsequence [1, 2, 3, 100].

Array: [3, 4, 5, 10]. Start at index 0 with last = -∞. Include 3: recurse from index 1 with last=3. Include 4: recurse from index 2 with last=4. Include 5: recurse from index 3 with last=5. Include 10: recurse from index 4 (end): return 0. Backtrack: sum = 10 + 0 = 10 at index 3, 5 + 10 = 15 at index 2, 4 + 15 = 19 at index 1, 3 + 19 = 22 at index 0. Maximum is 22 from subsequence [3, 4, 5, 10]. Skipping any element reduces sum.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsequences recursively, check if each is increasing, and track the maximum sum. Use it for small inputs to understand the problem but is inefficient for larger arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Generate all subsequences recursively, check if each is increasing, and track the maximum sum.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves the Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence (MSIS) problem by exploring all possible subsequences of the input array. The intuition is that by examining every subsequence, we guarantee finding the one with the maximum sum that is increasing. This is done through recursion: at each index, we decide whether to include the current element in the subsequence or skip it, but only include it if it maintains the increasing property relative to the last included element. We track the maximum sum found. The algorithm starts with a helper function that takes the current index and the last included element's value (or -∞ if none). At each step, if we include the current element (if it's greater than the last included), we recursively explore further with this element as the new last. If we skip it, we explore without including it. The base case is when we reach the end of the array, returning 0 (sum of an empty subsequence). The time complexity is O(2^n) because each element has two choices (include or skip), leading to 2^n subsequences. Space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion stack depth. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem but inefficient for large n. Connection to L1: This level implements the full recursion, while L1 focuses on the key recursive helper function. Common mistakes include not properly handling the initial last value (use -∞) or forgetting to track the maximum sum across choices. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function that returns the maximum sum from the current index onward, given the last value.

examples.trace: Array: [1, 101, 2, 3, 100]. Start at index 0 with last = -∞. Include 1 (since 1 > -∞): recurse from index 1 with last=1. At index 1, include 101 (101 > 1): recurse from index 2 with last=101. At index 2, skip 2 (2 not > 101), recurse from index 3 with last=101. At index 3, skip 3, recurse from index 4 with last=101. At index 4, skip 100, reach end: return 0. Backtrack: sum at index 4 = 0, index 3 = 0, index 2 = 0, index 1 = 101 + 0 = 101, index 0 = 1 + 101 = 102. Alternative at index 1: skip 101, recurse from index 2 with last=1. Include 2 (2 > 1): recurse from index 3 with last=2. Include 3 (3 > 2): recurse from index 4 with last=3. Include 100 (100 > 3): recurse from index 5 (end): return 0. Backtrack: sum = 100 + 0 = 100 at index 4, 3 + 100 = 103 at index 3, 2 + 103 = 105 at index 2, 0 at index 1 (skip), 1 + 105 = 106 at index 0. Maximum is 106 from subsequence [1, 2, 3, 100].

Array: [3, 4, 5, 10]. Start at index 0 with last = -∞. Include 3: recurse from index 1 with last=3. Include 4: recurse from index 2 with last=4. Include 5: recurse from index 3 with last=5. Include 10: recurse from index 4 (end): return 0. Backtrack: sum = 10 + 0 = 10 at index 3, 5 + 10 = 15 at index 2, 4 + 15 = 19 at index 1, 3 + 19 = 22 at index 0. Maximum is 22 from subsequence [3, 4, 5, 10]. Skipping any element reduces sum.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for MSIS
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes maximum sum from a given index with a last included value.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function used in the brute force approach. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems: from the current index onward, what is the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence, given the value of the last element included so far? The function takes two parameters: the current index i and the last value last (initialized to -∞ for no previous element). At each step, if i is at the end of the array, return 0 (base case). Otherwise, we have two choices: skip the current element (recurse with i+1 and same last) or include it if it is greater than last (recurse with i+1 and last = arr[i], adding arr[i] to the sum). The result is the maximum of these two choices. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^(n-i)) in worst case, but overall it contributes to O(2^n). Space is O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in L0 to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: not initializing last properly (use a very small number like -inf) or incorrect base case. Real code pattern: Define function solve(i, last) that returns an integer.

examples.trace: At i=0, last=-∞. Include 1 (1 > -∞): sum_include = 1 + solve(1, 1). At i=1, last=1. Include 2 (2 > 1): sum_include2 = 2 + solve(2, 2). At i=2 (end): return 0. So sum_include2 = 2 + 0 = 2. Back to i=1: skip option: solve(2, 1) = 0, max = max(2, 0) = 2. So sum_include = 1 + 2 = 3. Skip at i=0: solve(1, -∞) = from i=1, last=-∞: include 2 (2 > -∞) gives 2 + solve(2,2)=2, skip gives solve(2,-∞)=0, max=2. So solve(1,-∞)=2. Thus at i=0, max(3, 2) = 3.

At i=0, last=-∞. Include 5: sum_include = 5 + solve(1, 5). At i=1, last=5. Include 3 not allowed (3 not > 5), so only skip: solve(2, 5) = 0. Thus sum_include = 5 + 0 = 5. Skip at i=0: solve(1, -∞). At i=1, last=-∞: include 3 gives 3 + solve(2,3)=3, skip gives 0, max=3. So solve(1,-∞)=3. Thus at i=0, max(5, 3) = 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision to Include or Skip an Element in Recursion
ladders.desc: At each recursive step, decide whether to include the current element based on the increasing condition.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision-making logic within the recursion. The intuition is that to form an increasing subsequence, we can only include an element if it is greater than the last included element; otherwise, we must skip it. At each index i with last value last, we compute two values: include_sum (if arr[i] > last, then arr[i] + recursive call with last=arr[i]) and skip_sum (recursive call with same last). The result is the maximum of these two. This encapsulates the combinatorial exploration of subsequences. Time complexity for this decision is O(1) per call, but it drives the O(2^n) overall. Space is O(1) per call. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the helper function. Common mistakes: incorrectly comparing arr[i] to last (use > strictly) or not handling the initial last value. Real code pattern: if arr[i] > last: include = arr[i] + solve(i+1, arr[i]); skip = solve(i+1, last); return max(include, skip).

examples.input: arr[i]=3, last=2, next recursive results: include_path_sum=10, skip_path_sum=5
examples.trace: Given arr[i]=3, last=2. Since 3 > 2, we can include: include_sum = 3 + include_path_sum = 3 + 10 = 13. Skip_sum = skip_path_sum = 5. Maximum is max(13, 5) = 13.

examples.input: arr[i]=1, last=5, next recursive results: include_path_sum=0, skip_path_sum=8
examples.trace: Given arr[i]=1, last=5. Since 1 is not > 5, we cannot include, so include_sum is not considered (or set to -∞). Skip_sum = skip_path_sum = 8. Maximum is 8.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Exploration of Subsequences
ladders.desc: Understand how recursion generates all subsequences by including or skipping each element.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the recursive mechanism that generates all possible subsequences of an array. The intuition is that each element in the array can either be included in a subsequence or skipped, leading to 2^n total subsequences for an array of size n. Recursion simulates this by making two recursive calls at each step: one for including the current element and one for skipping it. The recursion tree has depth n (one level per element) and up to 2^n leaves. This is foundational for brute force solutions to subsequence problems. Time complexity is O(2^n) due to the exponential number of calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L2: This exploration uses the include/skip decision at each step. Common mistakes: not having a proper base case (when index equals array length) or incorrect recursion parameters. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function that takes an index and explores two paths.

examples.trace: Start at index 0. Path 1: skip A, go to index 1. At index 1: skip B -> subsequence []. Include B -> [B]. Path 2: include A, go to index 1 with A included. At index 1: skip B -> [A]. Include B -> [A, B]. Total: 4 subsequences.
Start at index 0. Skip X: go to index 1 (end) -> []. Include X: go to index 1 -> [X]. Total: 2 subsequences.

testCases.input: Array: [A, B, C], list subsequences of length 2
Array: [P, Q], list subsequences including P
Array: [A, B, C, D], count subsequences
Array: [X, Y, Z], list subsequences starting with X

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a Sequence is Increasing
ladders.desc: Determine if a given sequence of numbers is strictly increasing.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation checks whether a sequence (list or array) is strictly increasing, meaning each element is greater than the previous one. The intuition is to iterate through the sequence and compare each element with its predecessor; if any element is less than or equal to the previous, the sequence is not increasing. This is a fundamental condition for the MSIS problem. The algorithm: initialize a variable to track the last seen value (start with the first element or use a flag). For each subsequent element, if it is greater than the last, update last; otherwise, return false. If all checks pass, return true. Time complexity is O(k) for a sequence of length k. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This check is applied to each generated subsequence in the brute force approach. Common mistakes: using non-strict comparison (>= instead of >) or not handling empty sequences correctly. Real code pattern: Loop from index 1 to end, if arr[i] <= arr[i-1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Start with last = 1. Check 2 > 1: yes, update last=2. Check 3 > 2: yes. All checks pass, so True.
Start with last = 5. Check 3 > 5: no (3 <= 5), so return False immediately.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences
ladders.desc: Learn what a subsequence is and how it differs from subarrays and subsets.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces the concept of a subsequence. A subsequence of a sequence is a new sequence derived by deleting some (or none) of the elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. Key intuition: subsequences maintain the original order but not necessarily contiguity. For example, from [A, B, C], subsequences include [], [A], [B], [C], [A, B], [A, C], [B, C], [A, B, C]. This differs from a subarray (which must be contiguous) and a subset (which ignores order). The total number of subsequences for an array of length n is 2^n, because each element can be either included or excluded. This concept is crucial for problems involving sequences where we need to consider non-contiguous selections. Connection to L4: In MSIS, we check increasing property on subsequences. Common mistakes: confusing subsequence with subarray or subset. Real code pattern: To generate subsequences, use recursion or bit manipulation.

examples.input: Sequence: [X, Y, Z], list all subsequences
examples.trace: Consider each element: X: include or skip. Y: include or skip. Z: include or skip. Combinations: skip all -> []. Include only X -> [X]. Include only Y -> [Y]. Include only Z -> [Z]. Include X and Y -> [X, Y]. Include X and Z -> [X, Z]. Include Y and Z -> [Y, Z]. Include all -> [X, Y, Z]. Total 8 subsequences.
Original order is [1, 2]. To get [2, 1], we would need to reverse the order, which is not allowed in a subsequence. Subsequences must preserve order, so [2, 1] is not a subsequence of [1, 2].

testCases.input: Sequence: [A, B, C], list all subsequences
Sequence: [A, B, C, D], count subsequences
Sequence: [P, Q, R], is [Q, P] a subsequence?

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming Tabulation O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use a DP array where each element stores the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence ending at that index. Iterate and update based on previous elements. Suitable for medium-sized arrays due to its straightforward implementation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence using DP Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding the maximum sum of any strictly increasing subsequence in an array using O(n²) DP tabulation.
ladders.explanation: The maximum sum increasing subsequence (MSIS) problem requires finding the highest sum among all strictly increasing subsequences in a given array. The dynamic programming tabulation approach solves this by building a DP array where each element dp[i] stores the maximum sum of an increasing subsequence ending exactly at index i. The intuition is that for each element arr[i], we look at all previous elements arr[j] (where j < i) that are smaller than arr[i]. If arr[j] < arr[i], then arr[i] can extend the subsequence ending at j, potentially increasing the sum. We update dp[i] as max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]) for all such j. Initially, dp[i] is set to arr[i] since a subsequence can start at any element. After filling the DP array, the answer is the maximum value in dp[]. This works because it systematically considers all possible increasing subsequences by building them from left to right, ensuring optimal substructure—the best subsequence ending at i depends only on optimal solutions for previous indices. The algorithm runs in O(n²) time due to nested loops and uses O(n) space for the DP array. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize dp[i] with arr[i] (which handles single-element subsequences), incorrectly comparing elements (must be strictly increasing), or missing the final max operation over dp[]. This approach is straightforward and suitable for arrays up to a few thousand elements, though not optimal for very large n due to quadratic complexity.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Update for a Single Index
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum sum for an increasing subsequence ending at a specific index by checking all previous indices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on calculating dp[i], the maximum sum of any strictly increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i in the array. Given the array arr and the DP array dp (initialized with dp[j] = arr[j] for all j), the goal is to update dp[i] based on all previous indices j < i. The intuition is that if arr[j] < arr[i], then the element at i can extend the subsequence ending at j, forming a new subsequence with sum dp[j] + arr[i]. We want the maximum such sum, or keep dp[i] as arr[i] if no valid j exists (i.e., the subsequence starts at i). The algorithm iterates j from 0 to i-1, checks if arr[j] < arr[i], and if true, updates dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]). This ensures dp[i] stores the optimal sum for ending at i. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(i) in the worst case, contributing to the overall O(n²) when done for all i. Space is O(1) extra beyond input arrays. Connection to L0: This is the core inner loop in the full solution, executed for each i to build the DP array. Common mistakes include using ≤ instead of < for strict increase, not initializing dp[i] properly before the loop, or missing the max operation. Real code pattern: for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]). This sub-routine is independent and can be tested by fixing i and verifying dp[i] against manual computation.

examples.trace:
Given arr = [1, 101, 2, 3, 100, 4, 5], i = 4 (arr[4] = 100)
Initial dp[4] = 100 (from initialization)
Iterate j from 0 to 3:
j = 0: arr[0] = 1 < 100 → update dp[4] = max(100, dp[0]+100=1+100=101) = 101
j = 1: arr[1] = 101 not < 100 → skip
j = 2: arr[2] = 2 < 100 → update dp[4] = max(101, dp[2]+100=2+100=102) = 102
j = 3: arr[3] = 3 < 100 → update dp[4] = max(102, dp[3]+100=3+100=103) = 103
But wait, in full context, dp values might have been updated earlier. Assume dp from previous steps: dp = [1, 102, 3, 6, 100, ...] (as in L0 trace). Recompute with correct dp:
Initial dp[4] = 100
j = 0: 1 < 100, dp[0]=1 → dp[4] = max(100, 1+100=101) = 101
j = 1: 101 not < 100 → skip
j = 2: 2 < 100, dp[2]=3 → dp[4] = max(101, 3+100=103) = 103
j = 3: 3 < 100, dp[3]=6 → dp[4] = max(103, 6+100=106) = 106
Final dp[4] = 106

Given arr = [3, 4, 5, 10], i = 3 (arr[3] = 10)
Initial dp[3] = 10
Iterate j from 0 to 2:
Assume dp from previous steps: dp = [3, 7, 12, 10] (as in L0 trace).
j = 0: arr[0] = 3 < 10, dp[0]=3 → dp[3] = max(10, 3+10=13) = 13
j = 1: arr[1] = 4 < 10, dp[1]=7 → dp[3] = max(13, 7+10=17) = 17
j = 2: arr[2] = 5 < 10, dp[2]=12 → dp[3] = max(17, 12+10=22) = 22
Final dp[3] = 22

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure for Increasing Subsequences
ladders.desc: Understand how the best subsequence ending at an index depends on optimal solutions for previous indices.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the DP approach is based on optimal substructure: the maximum sum increasing subsequence ending at index i (denoted MSIS(i)) can be computed from MSIS(j) for all j < i where arr[j] < arr[i]. Specifically, MSIS(i) = arr[i] + max(MSIS(j)) over all j < i with arr[j] < arr[i], or simply arr[i] if no such j exists. This works because any increasing subsequence ending at i must have its second-to-last element at some j < i with arr[j] < arr[i]. By taking the maximum MSIS(j) and adding arr[i], we ensure the subsequence is increasing and has the highest possible sum up to i. The intuition is dynamic programming's principle of solving smaller subproblems first—here, computing MSIS for all indices from left to right builds up to the global maximum. This logic is implemented by maintaining a DP array where dp[i] stores MSIS(i). The algorithm initializes dp[i] = arr[i] (base case: subsequence of length 1), then for each i, iterates j from 0 to i-1 to update dp[i] using the recurrence. Time analysis: each dp[i] requires O(i) time, leading to O(n²) total. Space is O(n) for dp. Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the sub-routine to update a single dp[i]. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the strict increase condition or not handling the base case properly. Real code pattern: dp = arr.copy(); for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + arr[i]). This core insight is key to many DP problems involving sequences.

examples.trace:
Array: [1, 2, 3]
Initialize dp = [1, 2, 3]
i=0: MSIS(0)=1 → dp[0]=1
i=1: j=0, 1 < 2 → MSIS(1)=max(2, MSIS(0)+2=1+2=3)=3 → dp[1]=3
i=2: j=0, 1 < 3 → candidate: MSIS(0)+3=1+3=4; j=1, 2 < 3 → candidate: MSIS(1)+3=3+3=6; max among candidates and arr[2]=3 is 6 → MSIS(2)=6 → dp[2]=6
Final dp = [1, 3, 6]

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Previous Indices with Condition
ladders.desc: Loop through all indices before a given index and apply a condition to select valid predecessors.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental operation of iterating over all previous indices j from 0 to i-1 for a given index i in an array, and checking a condition—specifically, whether arr[j] < arr[i] for strictly increasing subsequences. The intuition is to explore all potential predecessors that can form an increasing sequence with the current element. In the context of MSIS, this loop identifies which previous elements can extend their subsequences to include arr[i]. The algorithm is straightforward: for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: then process further (e.g., update a sum). This requires O(i) time per i and O(1) space. Connection to L2: This iteration is used in the core logic to find all j that satisfy the increasing condition, enabling the update of dp[i]. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., using j <= i), incorrect comparison operators (e.g., using <= instead of < for non-decreasing), or not handling empty previous indices when i=0. Real code pattern: for j in range(i): if arr[j] < arr[i]: # do something. This building block is common in DP problems like longest increasing subsequence or maximum sum increasing subsequence, and can be practiced independently by writing a function that, given i, returns a list of valid j indices or counts them.

examples.trace:
Array: [1, 101, 2, 3, 100], i = 4
Iterate j from 0 to 3:
j = 0: arr[0] = 1, check 1 < 100 → true, include j=0
j = 1: arr[1] = 101, check 101 < 100 → false, skip
j = 2: arr[2] = 2, check 2 < 100 → true, include j=2
j = 3: arr[3] = 3, check 3 < 100 → true, include j=3
Valid j indices: [0, 2, 3]

Array: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1], i = 4
Iterate j from 0 to 3:
j = 0: arr[0] = 5, check 5 < 1 → false
j = 1: arr[1] = 4, check 4 < 1 → false
j = 2: arr[2] = 3, check 3 < 1 → false
j = 3: arr[3] = 2, check 2 < 1 → false
No valid j indices → []

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements for Strict Increase
ladders.desc: Check if one element is strictly less than another in an array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements in an array to determine if one is strictly less than the other. Specifically, given an array arr and two indices j and i, we check the condition arr[j] < arr[i]. This is fundamental for ensuring subsequences are strictly increasing, as required in the MSIS problem. The intuition is simple: in a strictly increasing sequence, each element must be greater than the previous one, so when considering extending a subsequence ending at j to include i, we must verify arr[j] < arr[i]. The operation is performed using a comparison operator (<) and returns a boolean. Time complexity is O(1), and space is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is the condition applied during iteration over previous indices to filter valid predecessors. Common mistakes include using <= (which allows equal elements, making the subsequence non-decreasing rather than strictly increasing) or comparing indices instead of values. Real code pattern: if arr[j] < arr[i]: # condition met. This operation can be practiced in isolation by writing a function that takes an array and two indices and returns True if arr[j] < arr[i], False otherwise. It's a building block for many algorithms involving order or sequences.

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 10, 3]
j = 0 → arr[0] = 5
i = 1 → arr[1] = 10
Check: 5 < 10 → True

Array: [5, 10, 3]
j = 1 → arr[1] = 10
i = 2 → arr[2] = 3
Check: 10 < 3 → False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value at a specific position in an array using its index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves accessing an element in an array by its index. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index—typically starting from 0 in most programming languages. To access the value at index i in an array arr, we use the syntax arr[i]. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] returns 10, arr[1] returns 20, and arr[2] returns 30. The intuition is that arrays provide direct, O(1) time access to any element based on its position, making them efficient for algorithms that require frequent lookups. This operation is basic but crucial for any algorithm that processes arrays, including the MSIS problem where we need to compare values at different indices. Time complexity is O(1), and space is O(1) for the access itself. Connection to L4: Accessing arr[j] and arr[i] is necessary to perform the comparison for strict increase. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (e.g., arr[1] for the first element) or accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[3] in a 3-element array), which can cause errors. Real code pattern: value = arr[index]. This can be practiced by writing simple functions that return the element at a given index or print all elements in an array using a loop. It's a prerequisite for understanding how algorithms traverse and manipulate data in sequences.

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 10, 15, 20]
Index 2 refers to the third element (since indices start at 0).
Access arr[2] → value is 15.

Array: [100, 200]
Index 0 refers to the first element.
Access arr[0] → value is 100.

approaches.name: Approach 3: DP with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain an array to track the smallest tail values for increasing subsequences of various lengths, using binary search to update efficiently. Optimizes time by reducing comparisons, ideal for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence using DP with Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete O(n log n) solution finding the maximum sum of any strictly increasing subsequence
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence (MSIS) problem optimally using dynamic programming enhanced with binary search. The intuition is to maintain an array 'tails' where tails[i] stores the smallest possible last element of an increasing subsequence of length i+1 that gives maximum sum up to that point. For each element in the input array, we use binary search to find where this element should be placed in the tails array. If the element is larger than all tails, we extend the array. If it's smaller than some existing tail, we replace it only if doing so gives a better (larger) sum for that subsequence length. We simultaneously maintain a 'sums' array where sums[i] tracks the maximum sum achievable for subsequences ending with tails[i]. The key insight is that by always keeping the smallest possible tail for each length, we ensure we can extend subsequences with future larger elements while maximizing sums. The algorithm processes each element once, performing binary search (O(log n)) for each, resulting in O(n log n) time complexity. Space complexity is O(n) for storing tails and sums arrays. This approach is particularly efficient for large inputs where O(n²) DP would be too slow. Common mistakes include not handling equal elements correctly (should be strictly increasing), forgetting to update both tails and sums arrays consistently, and incorrect binary search implementation that doesn't find the right insertion point. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the binary search subroutine with the DP framework to solve the complete problem.

examples.trace:
Input: [1, 101, 2, 3, 100, 4, 5]
Initialize tails = [], sums = []

Process 1: tails=[1], sums=[1]
Process 101: 101 > 1, append: tails=[1,101], sums=[1,102]
Process 2: binary search finds position 1 (2 > 1, 2 < 101), replace tails[1]=2, sums[1]=max(102,1+2)=3
  tails=[1,2], sums=[1,3]
Process 3: 3 > 2, append: tails=[1,2,3], sums=[1,3,6]
Process 100: binary search finds position 3 (100 > 3), append: tails=[1,2,3,100], sums=[1,3,6,106]
Process 4: binary search finds position 3 (4 > 3, 4 < 100), replace tails[3]=4, sums[3]=max(106,6+4)=10
  tails=[1,2,3,4], sums=[1,3,6,10]
Process 5: 5 > 4, append: tails=[1,2,3,4,5], sums=[1,3,6,10,15]
Maximum sum in sums = 106
Output: 106

Input: [3, 4, 5, 10]
Initialize tails = [], sums = []

Process 3: tails=[3], sums=[3]
Process 4: 4 > 3, append: tails=[3,4], sums=[3,7]
Process 5: 5 > 4, append: tails=[3,4,5], sums=[3,7,12]
Process 10: 10 > 5, append: tails=[3,4,5,10], sums=[3,7,12,22]
Maximum sum in sums = 22
Output: 22

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search in Dynamic Array for Optimal Placement
ladders.desc: Finding insertion position in sorted array using binary search for DP updates
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the engine of the O(n log n) MSIS solution. The intuition is that we maintain a dynamically growing sorted array (tails) representing the smallest possible last elements for increasing subsequences of various lengths. For each new element, we need to find where it belongs in this sorted array. We use binary search because the array is always sorted, giving us O(log n) search time instead of linear O(n) search. The binary search finds the leftmost position where the element is greater than or equal to the value at that position (for non-decreasing sequences) or strictly greater (for strictly increasing sequences). In MSIS, we need strictly increasing subsequences, so we find the position where the element is greater than the previous element but less than or equal to the current one. The algorithm uses standard binary search with careful boundary conditions: initialize left=0, right=len(tails), while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2, if tails[mid] < element: left = mid+1, else: right = mid. This gives us the insertion index. The key insight is that this binary search efficiently tells us whether to extend the array (if element > all tails) or replace an existing tail (if we found a position). This subroutine connects to L0 by being called for each of n elements, making the overall solution O(n log n). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in binary search bounds, incorrect comparison operators (using <= instead of < for strictly increasing), and not handling empty array case. Time complexity is O(log n) per search, space is O(1) for the search itself.

examples.trace:
tails = [1, 3, 6], element = 4
left=0, right=3
mid=(0+3)//2=1: tails[1]=3 < 4 → left=2
left=2, right=3
mid=(2+3)//2=2: tails[2]=6 >= 4 → right=2
left=2, right=2 → stop
Insertion position = 2
Explanation: 4 is greater than 3 but less than 6, so it should replace 6 at position 2

tails = [2, 5, 9], element = 10
left=0, right=3
mid=(0+3)//2=1: tails[1]=5 < 10 → left=2
left=2, right=3
mid=(2+3)//2=2: tails[2]=9 < 10 → left=3
left=3, right=3 → stop
Insertion position = 3
Explanation: 10 is greater than all elements, so it should be appended at position 3 (end of array)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Smallest Tails for Maximum Sum Subsequences
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming technique tracking optimal last elements for each subsequence length
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight that enables the O(n log n) solution. The intuition is that to find maximum sum increasing subsequences efficiently, we don't need to track all possible subsequences—we only need to track, for each possible length, the subsequence with that length that has both: (1) the smallest possible last element (to allow future extension with more elements), and (2) the maximum possible sum for that length. These two goals might seem contradictory, but they work together because by keeping the tail as small as possible while maximizing sum, we create opportunities to extend with larger elements later while maintaining high sums. We maintain two parallel arrays: 'tails' stores the actual last element values, and 'sums' stores the corresponding maximum sums. When processing a new element, we use binary search to find where it belongs in the tails array. If it's larger than all tails, we append it and calculate the new sum as the previous maximum sum plus the new element. If it replaces an existing tail, we only do so if the new sum (sum from previous position + new element) is greater than the current sum at that position. This ensures we always have the optimal configuration for each length. The key insight is that this greedy replacement strategy works because any future element that could extend a subsequence ending with the old tail could also extend one ending with a smaller or equal tail with equal or better sum. This logic connects to L1 by providing the context for why we perform binary search—to find the right position in this optimized tails array. Common mistakes include not maintaining sums array properly, replacing tails even when it doesn't improve the sum, or incorrectly calculating the new sum when appending. Time complexity for maintaining these arrays is O(n) overall when combined with binary search.

examples.trace:
Current state: tails=[1,4], sums=[1,5]
Element to process: 3
Binary search finds position 1 (3 > 1, 3 < 4)
Check if we should replace tails[1]=4 with 3:
  New sum = sums[0] + 3 = 1 + 3 = 4
  Current sum at position 1 = 5
  Since 4 < 5, we do NOT replace
  tails remains [1,4], sums remains [1,5]

Alternative scenario if sums[0]=2:
  Current state: tails=[1,4], sums=[2,5]
  New sum = 2 + 3 = 5
  Current sum = 5
  Since 5 >= 5, we replace: tails=[1,3], sums=[2,5]
  Now we have smaller tail (3 instead of 4) with same sum, allowing better future extensions

Current state: tails=[1,3,5], sums=[1,4,9]
Element to process: 6
Binary search: 6 > 5, so position = 3 (append)
Calculate new sum = sums[2] + 6 = 9 + 6 = 15
Append 6 to tails: tails=[1,3,5,6]
Append 15 to sums: sums=[1,4,9,15]
Now we have length 4 subsequence with sum 15

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming for Subsequence Problems
ladders.desc: Foundational DP approach to solving subsequence optimization problems
ladders.explanation: Dynamic programming is essential for solving subsequence problems efficiently by breaking them into overlapping subproblems. The intuition for subsequence DP is that we can build solutions incrementally: for each position in the array, we consider the best result ending at that position, then combine these to find the global optimum. For increasing subsequence problems specifically, the standard DP approach defines dp[i] as the maximum sum (or length) of an increasing subsequence ending at position i. We compute dp[i] by looking at all previous positions j < i where arr[j] < arr[i], and take the maximum dp[j] + arr[i]. This gives an O(n²) solution. The key insight is that this has optimal substructure—the solution to dp[i] depends on solutions to smaller subproblems dp[j]. We also have overlapping subproblems because multiple dp[i] values might consider the same dp[j]. This building block connects to L2 by showing the basic DP framework that the O(n log n) solution optimizes. The O(n log n) approach still uses DP principles but optimizes the 'looking at all previous positions' step using binary search and the tails array. Common mistakes in basic subsequence DP include incorrect base cases (dp[0] should usually be arr[0]), forgetting the condition arr[j] < arr[i] for increasing subsequences, and not considering the subsequence consisting of just the element itself as a valid option. Time complexity is O(n²), space is O(n) for the dp array. This building block is crucial for understanding why we need optimization and how the more advanced approach improves upon it.

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 10, 2, 1, 20]
Initialize dp same as array: [3, 10, 2, 1, 20]

i=0: dp[0]=3 (base case)
i=1: Look at j=0: arr[0]=3 < 10, dp[1]=max(10, dp[0]+10=13)=13
i=2: Look at j=0,1:
  j=0: 3 < 2? No
  j=1: 10 < 2? No
  dp[2]=2 (no valid previous elements)
i=3: Look at j=0,1,2:
  j=0: 3 < 1? No
  j=1: 10 < 1? No
  j=2: 2 < 1? No
  dp[3]=1
i=4: Look at j=0,1,2,3:
  j=0: 3 < 20, candidate=dp[0]+20=23
  j=1: 10 < 20, candidate=dp[1]+20=33
  j=2: 2 < 20, candidate=dp[2]+20=22
  j=3: 1 < 20, candidate=dp[3]+20=21
  dp[4]=max(20,23,33,22,21)=33

Final dp=[3,13,2,1,33], maximum=33

Array: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]
Initialize dp: [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

i=0: dp[0]=5
i=1: Look at j=0: 5 < 4? No → dp[1]=4
i=2: Look at j=0,1: 5<3? No, 4<3? No → dp[2]=3
i=3: Look at j=0,1,2: all comparisons false → dp[3]=2
i=4: Look at j=0,1,2,3: all comparisons false → dp[4]=1

Final dp=[5,4,3,2,1], maximum=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Search on Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Finding elements or insertion positions in sorted arrays using divide-and-conquer
ladders.explanation: Binary search is a fundamental algorithm for efficiently searching in sorted arrays. The intuition is simple: instead of checking every element (O(n)), we repeatedly divide the search space in half by comparing the target with the middle element. For a sorted array of size n, we can find an element or determine where it should be inserted in O(log n) time. The algorithm maintains two pointers: 'left' (inclusive start) and 'right' (exclusive end). While left < right, compute mid = (left + right) // 2. Compare the target with array[mid]. If target equals array[mid], we've found it. If target is less, search the left half by setting right = mid. If target is greater, search the right half by setting left = mid + 1. When left == right, we've found the insertion position. For finding insertion position (rather than exact match), we typically want the leftmost position where the element is greater than or equal to the target. This basic operation connects to L3 by being the optimization that turns O(n²) DP into O(n log n)—instead of linearly scanning all previous elements, we use binary search on the tails array. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bounds (using <= instead of < in the loop), integer overflow when computing mid (though not an issue in Python), forgetting to update bounds correctly, and not handling empty arrays. Time complexity is O(log n), space is O(1). This operation is crucial not just for MSIS but for many optimization problems where we maintain sorted structures.

examples.trace:
Array: [1,3,5,7,9], target=5
left=0, right=5
mid=(0+5)//2=2: array[2]=5 == target → found at index 2

For insertion position (if not found):
left=0, right=5
mid=2: 5 >= 5 → right=2
left=0, right=2
mid=1: 3 < 5 → left=2
left=2, right=2 → insertion position 2

Array: [2,4,6,8], target=5
Find insertion position:
left=0, right=4
mid=(0+4)//2=2: array[2]=6 >= 5 → right=2
left=0, right=2
mid=1: array[1]=4 < 5 → left=2
left=2, right=2 → insertion position 2
Explanation: 5 should be inserted at index 2, between 4 and 6

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences and Increasing Order
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of sequences, subsequences, and increasing order relations
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers the absolute basics needed to understand the MSIS problem. A sequence is an ordered list of elements, like [3,1,4,2]. A subsequence is obtained by deleting zero or more elements from the original sequence without changing the order of remaining elements. For example, from [3,1,4,2], some subsequences are: [3,4], [1,2], [3,1,2], and even [] (empty) and the full sequence itself. The key is order preservation—[4,1] is NOT a subsequence of [3,1,4,2] because 4 comes before 1 in the subsequence but after 1 in the original. An increasing subsequence is one where each element is strictly greater than the previous one. For example, in [3,1,4,2], the increasing subsequences include [1,4], [1,2], [3,4], but not [3,1] (decreasing) or [1,4,2] (4>1 but 2<4). Strictly increasing means no equal elements allowed. The maximum sum increasing subsequence is simply the increasing subsequence with the largest sum of its elements. For [3,1,4,2], the increasing subsequences and their sums are: [3]=3, [1]=1, [4]=4, [2]=2, [3,4]=7, [1,4]=5, [1,2]=3. The maximum sum is 7 from [3,4]. This foundation connects to L4 by establishing what problem we're trying to solve—without understanding what a subsequence is and what 'increasing' means, we can't even understand the problem statement. Common beginner mistakes include confusing subsequences with subarrays (which must be contiguous), not understanding that order must be preserved, and missing that elements can be skipped. No algorithms needed at this level—just conceptual understanding.

examples.trace:
Original sequence: [5, 2, 8, 6]
Single-element subsequences (always increasing): [5], [2], [8], [6]
Two-element increasing subsequences:
  Start with 5: 5,8 (5<8) ✓, 5,6 (5<6) ✓
  Start with 2: 2,8 (2<8) ✓, 2,6 (2<6) ✓
  Start with 8: no elements after 8 are >8
Three-element: 5,8,6 not increasing (8>6 fails)
  Other attempts: 2,8,6 fails (8>6)
Four-element: full sequence not increasing (8>6 fails)
So all increasing subsequences: [5],[2],[8],[6],[5,8],[5,6],[2,8],[2,6]
Note: [5,6] is valid even though 5 and 6 aren't consecutive in original

examples.output: All strictly increasing subsequences: [1],[2],[1,2] (first or second 1)
examples.trace:
Original sequence: [1, 1, 2]
Strictly increasing means each element must be GREATER than previous, not equal.
Single-element: [1] (from first position), [1] (from second), [2]
Two-element:
  First 1 then second 1: 1=1, not greater → not strictly increasing
  First 1 then 2: 1<2 ✓ → [1,2] using first 1
  Second 1 then 2: 1<2 ✓ → [1,2] using second 1
Three-element: 1,1,2 fails because 1=1
So strictly increasing subsequences: [1],[1],[2],[1,2],[1,2]
Note: There are two different [1,2] subsequences using different 1's
